ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singaporean roasted pork rice (siu yuk fan). Mapped to closest parent class 'pork dish' (FOODON_03315671) as the most defensible FoodOn anchor available.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for the roasted pork component of this rice dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific composite code for roasted pork rice. A0FJP (cooked pork) is used as the closest parent anchor for the primary protein component; the rice component is not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for roasted pork rice as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of roasted pork rice as served at a Singapore hawker centre typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising steamed white rice topped with sliced roasted pork belly and a ladle of soy-based gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Pork Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Roasted Pork Rice (烧肉饭), cr_id G00010702. All per-100g macros and available micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pork belly roasted data (USDA SR Legacy #10006) proportionally adjusted to the dish fat content of 4.21 g/100g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white rice (steamed, Asian-style) GI = 72
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite dish 'Roasted Pork Rice'. The GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component — steamed white rice (Asian-style) — as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The pork and gravy components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 88.2) / 100 ≈ 63.5, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 25.19 × 3.5 = 88.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g (Na:K ratio < 1)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced gravy and remove pork crackling to limit sodium intake; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio provides partial mitigation, but total sodium per 350 g serving (~809 mg) approaches one-third of the 2,300 mg daily limit and portion control is essential.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice (GI 72) with 25.19 g/100g carbohydrate produces a high glycaemic load of ~63.5 per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or reduce portion size to half a plate, add non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption, and remove pork skin to reduce saturated fat that worsens insulin resistance; the high glycaemic load of this dish makes it unsuitable for regular consumption without these modifications.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from roasted pork belly and crackling; sodium 231 mg/100g; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100g in HPB source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should remove the pork crackling (skin) to reduce saturated fat intake, request minimal gravy to control sodium, and limit portion frequency to occasional consumption given the saturated fat and sodium burden of roasted pork belly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Roasted pork belly is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6); high-temperature roasting generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that amplify joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit or avoid roasted pork belly due to its high arachidonic acid content and AGE burden from high-heat roasting; substituting with grilled chicken breast and reducing gravy sodium are recommended modifications.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Pork Rice (烧肉饭, Siu Yuk Fan) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of steamed white rice topped with slices of siu yuk — Cantonese-style roasted pork belly characterised by its crackling crispy skin, tender layered meat, and rich fat. The dish is typically served with a ladle of dark soy-based gravy and sometimes accompanied by blanched vegetables or pickled mustard greens. The pork belly is marinated with five-spice powder, salt, and rice wine, then roasted at high temperature to achieve the signature blistered, crackling skin. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with meaningful dietary fibre at 3.7 g and potassium at 327 mg. The sodium content of 231 mg/100g is moderate but accumulates significantly over a standard 350 g serving (~809 mg). The dish carries a high glycaemic index of approximately 72 driven by the steamed white rice base, making it a high-glycaemic-load meal unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat from pork belly and crackling raises cardiovascular concern. Clinically, the dish is best modified by removing the pork skin, substituting brown rice, and requesting reduced gravy. It is a staple lunch and dinner option at Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops, deeply embedded in Singaporean Chinese food culture.
common_user_queries: Is 烧肉饭 suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat roasted pork rice if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in a plate of siu yuk rice?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010702, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and key micronutrients are present. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork belly data and clearly documented. GI is assigned from the University of Sydney GI Database via the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables using the dominant carbohydrate component (steamed white rice, GI 72).
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #10006 (pork belly, raw) adjusted proportionally to the dish fat content of 4.21 g/100g; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for these nutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for Roasted Pork Rice (G00010702) as of April 2026, and no approved secondary source provides composite-dish-level values for this specific preparation: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB for this composite dish entry; vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg — full vitamin panel not reported by HPB for this composite dish entry; phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg — trace mineral data not reported by HPB for this composite dish entry; water_g — moisture content not reported by HPB for this composite dish entry. Source consulted and confirmed unavailability: HPB Singapore Foodo database (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/), accessed 2026-04-16. 3. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100g in the HPB source; this is an unusually low value for a pork-containing dish and may reflect a measurement or reporting artefact in the HPB record — confirmed zero per HPB source, preserved as reported. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on the steamed white rice component (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008); no direct GI measurement exists for the composite dish. 5. The dish is not halal as it contains pork. 6. Ontology match types set to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact composite-dish codes exist for roasted pork rice in either system.
